county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine counties of Montana Territory. Bannack, the first territorial capital, is in Beaverhead County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Beaverhead County Clerk and Recorder in Dillon holds deed and land records from 1865. Bannack was Montana's first territorial capital (1862-65) and a gold rush boomtown — early mining district records survive at the Montana Historical Society (MHS) in Helena. The Beaverhead County Museum in Dillon has pioneer family files and photographs. Grasshopper Creek gold discovery (1862) predates county formation; check territorial records at MHS. Ranching dominated after the gold rush — livestock brand books at MHS identify early stockmen. Check the University of Montana archives for early territorial census fragments. The Beaverhead National Forest records at NARA Seattle document homesteaders and timber claims in the mountain valleys.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1865 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1865. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

Civil and criminal case files, probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, and name changes from 1865.
Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Beaverhead County Clerk and Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Beaverhead County. Montana Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.
Historical newspapers from Beaverhead County communities. Montana Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Montana papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from parts of Rosebud and Yellowstone counties. Site of the Battle of the Little Bighorn (1876). Contains the Crow Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Big Horn County Clerk and Recorder in Hardin maintains deed records from 1913. The Battle of the Little Bighorn (1876) is the county's most famous event — military records at NARA document the 7th Cavalry and related campaigns. Crow Indian Reservation covers much of the county; BIA agency records at NARA Denver include census rolls, allotment records, and school attendance files. The Big Horn County Historical Museum in Hardin has homestead-era photographs and family files. Check Rosebud and Yellowstone counties for pre-1913 records. The Crow Agency maintains tribal enrollment records. Northern Pacific Railroad brought settlers; railroad land grant records at MHS identify purchasers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1912 from Chouteau County. Contains part of Fort Belknap Indian Reservation (Assiniboine and Gros Ventre).
county_metadata.research_tips: Blaine County Clerk and Recorder in Chinook holds records from 1912. Fort Belknap Indian Reservation (Assiniboine and Gros Ventre tribes) covers the southern portion — BIA agency records at NARA Denver include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, and school files. Chief Joseph and the Nez Perce surrendered near Chinook in 1877 (Bear Paw Battlefield); military records at NARA document this campaign. The Blaine County Museum in Chinook has homestead-era records. Check Chouteau County for pre-1912 records. Fort Assiniboine military post records (1879-1911) at NARA document soldiers stationed in the area. Homesteading boom (1909-1918) brought thousands of settlers; many claims were later abandoned during the drought years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1912 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1897 from parts of Jefferson and Meagher counties. Named for Col. Charles Broadwater, railroad builder.
county_metadata.research_tips: Broadwater County Clerk and Recorder in Townsend has deed and land records from 1897. Canton Valley and the Missouri River valley attracted ranchers and farmers. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the county, and railroad land records identify early settlers. Broadwater County Museum in Townsend holds local family files and photographs. Check Jefferson and Meagher counties for pre-1897 records. The nearby Confederate Gulch gold strike (1864) in the Big Belt Mountains drew miners whose records may appear in early territorial documents at MHS. Canyon Ferry Dam (1954) flooded some early settlement sites; pre-dam homestead records at BLM's GLO database are important for tracking displaced families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1895 from parts of Park and Yellowstone counties. Named for coal deposits.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carbon County Clerk and Recorder in Red Lodge holds records from 1895. Red Lodge was a major coal mining town attracting Finnish, Slavic, Italian, Scottish, and Scandinavian immigrants — mine company employment records and ethnic fraternal society records are at the Carbon County Historical Society. The Beartooth Highway and Yellowstone access shaped tourism. Check Park and Yellowstone counties for pre-1895 records. The 1943 Smith Mine disaster in Washoe killed 74 miners; coroner's records and mine safety reports identify victims and families. Red Lodge newspapers (Red Lodge Picket, Carbon County News) are excellent genealogical sources available on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Fallon County. One of Montana's most remote and least-populated counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carter County Clerk and Recorder in Ekalaka maintains records from 1917. Carter County is extremely remote and sparsely populated — the Carter County Museum in Ekalaka holds local ranching family files and paleontological collections. Homesteaders arrived during the 1909-1918 boom, but most claims failed during the drought of the 1920s and 1930s; abandoned homestead records at BLM's GLO database document these families. Check Fallon County for pre-1917 records. Livestock brand records at the Montana Department of Livestock identify ranching families. The county's isolation means local newspaper archives (the Ekalaka Eagle) are particularly valuable for family history research.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1887 from parts of Chouteau and Meagher counties. Great Falls became a major city due to hydroelectric power and the Anaconda Copper smelter.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cascade County Clerk and Recorder in Great Falls has deed records from 1887. Great Falls was founded by Paris Gibson in 1883 and became Montana's second-largest city. The Anaconda Copper Mining Company smelter employed thousands — company employment records at MHS and the University of Montana archives. Malmstrom Air Force Base (1942+) military personnel records at NARA. The History Museum in Great Falls has extensive local genealogy collections. The Great Falls Genealogy Society maintains surname files and cemetery indexes. Great Falls Tribune newspaper archives (1885+) are on microfilm at the Great Falls Public Library. Fort Benton in neighboring Chouteau County was the head of Missouri River steamboat navigation — related records document early Cascade County settlers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Fort Benton was the head of steamboat navigation on the Missouri River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chouteau County Clerk and Recorder in Fort Benton holds some of Montana's earliest records, dating to 1865. Fort Benton was the most important Missouri River port in Montana — steamboat passenger manifests, fur trade records, and I.G. Baker Company records at MHS document early arrivals. Chouteau County is the parent of many other Montana counties. The Fort Benton Museum of the Upper Missouri has outstanding fur trade and early settlement collections. American Fur Company records at the Missouri Historical Society (St. Louis) cover the Fort Benton trading post. Homesteading boom (1909-1918) brought thousands to the bench lands; many claims failed during drought. Check the Overholser Historical Research Center in Fort Benton for river trade family records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Named for George Armstrong Custer after the Battle of the Little Bighorn (1876).
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County Clerk and Recorder in Miles City has records from 1865. Miles City was the center of Montana's open-range cattle industry — the Montana Stockgrowers Association records at MHS document ranchers, brands, and roundup records. Fort Keogh (est. 1877) military records at NARA cover soldiers and Indian scouts. The Range Riders Museum in Miles City has extensive ranching family collections. Custer County is the parent county for much of eastern Montana. The Miles City Star newspaper (1881+) on microfilm at MHS is an exceptional genealogical source for southeastern Montana families. Texas trail drives brought cattle and cowboys to the Yellowstone valley in the 1880s; trail boss journals at MHS identify drovers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1920 from Valley County. Named for Mansfield A. Daniels, a local rancher.
county_metadata.research_tips: Daniels County Clerk and Recorder in Scobey has records from 1920. The Daniels County Museum and Pioneer Town in Scobey preserves homestead-era buildings and family records. Homesteaders arrived in large numbers during the 1909-1918 boom, many of Scandinavian descent from Minnesota and the Dakotas. The Great Northern Railway brought settlers along the Hi-Line corridor. Check Valley County for pre-1920 records. Many homestead claims were abandoned during the drought and depression of the 1920s-1930s; proof-of-claim documents at NARA and BLM's GLO database are essential for tracing these families. The Daniels County Free Press newspaper is on microfilm at MHS.
